passage:
A student is writing an essay about the economic consequences of urban tree canopy. The student has taken the following notes:
- Studies find that properties within 100 feet of mature street trees sell for 10–15% more than comparable properties without trees.
- Trees reduce building cooling costs by providing shade, lowering air conditioning energy use by up to 25% in adjacent buildings.
- A 2017 study estimated that urban trees in the United States provide approximately $18.3 billion in annual energy savings.
- Trees reduce urban heat island effects, lowering ambient air temperatures in densely built areas by 2–8°F.
- Municipal tree planting and maintenance programs often have benefit-to-cost ratios exceeding 3:1.
question: Which choice most effectively uses the notes to make the case that urban tree canopy represents a fiscally sound public investment?

choices.A: Trees are nice to have in cities, and some studies suggest they can save energy.
choices.B: Urban street trees increase property values by 10–15%, reduce building cooling costs by up to 25%, and contribute to an estimated $18.3 billion in annual national energy savings—benefits that help explain why municipal tree programs regularly return more than three dollars for every dollar spent.
choices.C: Urban trees lower air temperatures in cities and can reduce energy use, which is why many cities plant them.
choices.D: Property values are affected by many factors, including proximity to trees, so cities should plant more trees.

explanation: B integrates three specific quantified benefits (property value increase, cooling cost reduction, national energy savings) and connects them logically to the fiscal efficiency measure (3:1+ benefit-to-cost ratio), building a coherent argument for public investment. A is vague and unspecific, using no data from the notes. C mentions temperature reduction and energy use generally but omits property values, the energy savings figure, and the benefit-to-cost ratio. D frames the property value note as a subordinate clause and reaches the conclusion (cities should plant trees) without synthesizing the economic evidence from the notes.
